comments.title: Is it real?
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I grew up in a conservative Christian environment. When I was very young I vividly remember religious leaders say “God told me”, or “I was shown by the Lord”. It made me want to become a pastor, because I wanted this close experience myself. As I grew older I realised this was mostly sincere self-delusion on the part of the church leaders, and I was disappointed.

My question, and probably concern is that the same is true of my new journey into magic. I read some fantastical personal tales of interactions with angels and deities on this forum and I can’t help but wonder if it’s real or sincere self delusion?

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: Well, what do you mean by "real"? That's a more complex and nuanced word than a lot of people realize.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: I guess what I mean by real is an experience which I would be able to recognize as external to my own fantasy, like encountering a consciousness which is separate from my own.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content:
But whether you recognize something as external to your own fantasy, and whether you accept the presence of another consciousness behind the faces of the people you meet, is something you choose, not something that's forced on you by circumstances. The imagination, according to magical philosophy, is a means of perception and action on the subtle planes of being; it's always possible, no matter what happens, to insist that something you perceive that way is "just your imagination" -- and of course people do insist this.

You mention that you came to see the religious experiences of the pastors you knew as "sincere self-delusion." How do you know this? Was it simply that their claims didn't turn out to be correct about certain things?

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: I think it is just the pastors' way of talking to suggest that he interprets the Bible a certain way and that it is the Word of God. By saying 'God spoke to him', he basically just means that he read the Bible.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: This is the legacy of the "sola scriptura" doctrine which emerged during the Reformation. It enables any uninitiated schlub off the street to stand up on a pulpit and pretend they are an expert on metaphysics via the decidedly-unspiritual act of reading the Bible like a lawyer consults old case law -- all-the-while making bold and presumptuous claims of having spiritual experiences. LOL.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content:
Wait... there has to be a difference between 'real' and 'not real'. As in, our imaginations can tell us all we want to know about a future full of space travel and limitless energy, but reality will serve us according to its rules, not according to our fantasy.

I've had experiences that involved the unmistakable feeling that a non-human consciousness was looking back at me, as well as seeming 'encounters' that upon reflection seemed to have more to do with me projecting my own wishes onto the world. There seems to be a long and vague spectrum between the two. I find it tiring/worrisome trying to discern the difference (or possibly gradient) between a consciousness outside my own and a manifestation of my own unconscious.

Could you help us out with some distinctions here?

-Dylan

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: The problem here is that the term "real" has become loaded with a vast burden of emotions, assumptions, and begged questions. If you want to say "this doesn't happen on the material plane," I won't quibble with that at all; if you want to say "there was another consciousness involved in the interaction," that's a useful data point. It's trying to shoehorn all this into the arbitrary distinction "real" vs. "unreal" that's problematix,. Your unconscious exists, doesn't it? So phenomena generated by your unconscious also exist, don't they? That being the case, what makes them unreal?

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content:
Let me try to plug this back into what I've learned so far from your blogs and books, leaving the inadequate word in quotes.

Magical practice is useful because it provides:
1) Strengthening and stabilizing of the subtle bodies so as to experience more of 'reality' than one otherwise could
2) Mental models that map out the various levels and complexities of 'reality' so that the practitioner can actually grok those experiences
3) A symbolic language with which to communicate directly with 'reality' and understand its character

These three functions correspond to the practices of ritual, meditation, and divination, respectively. Please let me know if I've left out something significant.

What I'm stuck on is the little thing we call social reality. My experiences of the mystical thus far have made me lonelier, on the whole, because I can't seem to find people with whom to talk about them. I want to deepen my experience and understanding, but I fear that taking up a magical correspondence course or further solitary practice will only move me further into my existential solitude, without leading to more in-person friendships. Any advice for a struggling seeker?

Dylan

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: There's a reason why Manly P. Hall's book of stories about the path of the occultist is called The Ways of the Lonely Ones. The one upside is that as things proceed, you'll end up meeting people who are also on the path, and some very rich friendships happen that way.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: That's the whole point about faith - you have to choose to believe even though there could be other, more "rational", explanations for what we are experiencing. So much of what we do is shaped by our own beliefs about what is "real", because it's taking place largely in our own minds. The more we are willing to suspend disbelief and proceed on the basis that what we are experiencing is real, the more finely our perception, our instincts, and our intuition develop, and the deeper and richer (and more undeniable) our experiences become.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content:
How about: "Reality is that which, when you stop believing in it, doesn't go away" (Kindred Dick)? (A hypothesis: "verifiable by unbeliever".)

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content:
As an ex-atheist, let me tell you that much of what we consider real or not-real is a matter of interpretation.

If we were to take everything we know and take out everything that isn't a pure fact, we'd be left with an awful lot of empty space and disconnected facts. We fill up that empty space and make connections between facts by coming up with explanations for how those facts fit together.

Suppose you hear a voice in your head that doesn't seem to be yours. The facts in this case are that you hear a voice and that it doesn't seem to be yours. You can take these facts and interpret them as being something purely internal to your mind, or you can interpret them as being the result of some outside being communicating directly with your mind.

Since both explanations fit the facts equally well, we have no way of knowing whether either interpretation is correct–just like we have no way of knowing whether or not the whole world is real, a dream, or a computer simulation. In cases like these all we can really do is go with whatever is most intuitive to us.

You can see this happening in a scientific context with the various interpretations of quantum mechanics. Since there are multiple interpretations which fit the known facts equally well, people just go with the one that appeals to them most based on whatever criteria.

replies.title: Re: Is it real?
replies.content: Nicely put. Thank you.

comments.title: This may have been asked before
comments.content: Apologies if this has been asked before; I don't see a search feature to check.  I am curious what, if anything, Occultism has to say about abortion.

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content: According to occult tradition the soul does not enter into incarnation in the body until the point of quickening, which usually happens 13 to 16 weeks into the pregnancy. Something like half of all pregnancies spontaneously end before that point -- according to occult teachings, that means that no soul needed that body -- and before quickening, an induced abortion is a very minor thing. Afterwards it's a more serious matter, and though it's traditionally up to the mother and nobody else whether to carry a child to term, induced abortion after quickening is discouraged by the traditional lore except in cases where the mother's safety is at risk, or when some other serious factor such as rape or incest is involved.

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content:
"traditionally up to the mother and nobody else whether to carry a child to term" - do you mean "according to Western occult tradition"? If yes, is the purpose of it having an opinion on that directly-pragmatic, social-customs-aimed, or both?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content: To be fair, this is something about which different branches of the Western occult tradition disagree. The purpose of the teaching is, as with so many other aspects of occult moral teaching, to encourage people to focus on their own ethical decisions and avoid poking their noses into other people's business.

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content:
If you don't mind, Mr. Greer, I have a follow-up question. What does it mean that no soul needed that body? Is that a soul needs to be "available", as in close in space or close in time, or some other factor or combination of factors?

In the works of William S. Burroughs, specifically the book "The Western Lands", he talks about there being seven different souls with a given body, and that if one or the other of the souls feels like the body isn't being effective or working out, that it can leave before bodily death, leaving the body "soulless" in one way or another. Is this just a fantasy he came up with or is there an esoteric underpinning to this idea?

Thank you!

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content: Each new body brings with it a specific set of experiences, conditioned by the parents, their social position, such of their destinies as has already been determined, and so on. If there doesn't happen to be a soul that needs that particular set of experiences for the purposes of its further development. As far as Burroughs, well, I tend to think he was using way too many drugs.

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content: Now, this would seem to suggest that spontaneous abortions should be more common now, given the absurdly large human population. Do you know if there's any evidence for this?

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content: Keep in mind that the supply of human bodies is making up for the sharp decline in the supply of available bodies for relatively intelligent nonhuman animals. Thus it's by no means obvious that the rate of spontaneous abortions would go up.

replies.title: abortion
replies.content:
I have heard that at least a majority of Jewish authorities teach that the soul does not enter until birth, relying on the scriptures Genesis 2:5 in which Adam becomes a living being when God breathes into him. Therefore the child is not ensouled until it takes a breath. Jewish law also treats the life of the mother as of greater importance than that of the fetus--an abortion is required, not merely permitted, if necessary to save the mother.

I recall that one of Stuart Farrar's books addressed the question from a Wiccan POV. He claimed to have performed a magical abortion, but emphasized that it was not to be done casually.

Rita

replies.title: Re: abortion
replies.content:
(Deborah Bender. I forgot to log in before writing this.)

I got information about Judaism's views on abortion from a pamphlet distributed at a pro-choice demonstration years ago. It included the information about the lives of people already born, in this instance life of the mother, being more important than the life of a baby who isn't born yet. Consequently, abortion to save the life of the mother is not only permitted but required right up until the moment the baby's head emerges from the birth canal.

At that moment (if it is alive and can take a breath), it is a separate living human being, and killing it would be infanticide. Jewish law does not permit murdering one innocent person to save another, the test case in the Talmud being a mob gathered outside a building and demanding that the people inside send out one of their number to be lynched.

Miscarriages are not treated as human deaths in Jewish law. Perhaps they would be if women were rabbis during the time the Talmud was being compiled, but there is no period of formal mourning for a miscarriage and no ceremony attached to the event.

According to what I recollect from the pamphlet, it's not so much that a majority of Jewish authorities cite Genesis 2:5, although it may well be that they do, but rather that the Talmudic rabbis were not inclined to give rulings about Jewish law on the basis of concepts that cannot be proven or disproven, such as the exact moment when the soul enters the body. Since that is a question on which differences of opinion are possible, the more basic principle that governs is that saving human lives is paramount over most other religious requirements (such as keeping kosher or not lighting a fire on the sabbath).

This doesn't equate to abortion on demand. Torah Judaism encourages people to marry and have children.

replies.title: Re: This may have been asked before
replies.content:
(Not a question)...

It is interesting to me that this would seem to be the kind of thinking that informs abortion law in many European countries (not the UK, though), and is shortly to become law in Ireland. An abortion up to 12 weeks is available in many of these countries to anyone who seeks one, without having to explain or justify. (and the vast majority of induced abortions do, in fact, take place before week 12). In later gestational ages various tests having to do with safety and health of mother and baby are generally applied, although these vary from country to country.

Most people seem to find this kind of nuance, where the pregnancy, and its implications, are seen to change from one trimester to the next, sensible, although small minorities at either extreme of this issue, argue strongly either for complete restriction or for no restriction on abortion.

This teaching makes considerable intuitive sense, I think, to most people. (And used to inform church teaching, also, which is why many religious Catholics in Ireland were able to vote to permit this kind of law to be written here).

replies.content: Cold forged iron is hammered without being heated, and it has a potent disruptive effect on etheric fields. Any object of iron or steel has the same effect to a lesser degree. It's useful if you need serious protection, but yes, it has some drawbacks if you deal with human sensitives or certain nonphysical beings.

replies.title: Re: Cold Iron?
replies.content: Does any piece of iron become cold iron if you shape it by cold forging, or must it somehow never have been heated?

replies.title: Re: Cold Iron?
replies.content: All iron is heated -- you have to smelt the ore at a fairly high temperature to get metallic iron rather than ore! It's the process of cold forging that gives it the special etheric properties in question.

comments.title: Reincarnation continued
comments.content:
Hello JMG, LunarApprentice here.
One again, thank you for your kind response last Monday…

I had several questions regarding reincarnation, and also mentioned my older daughter’s feelings of attachment to my departed farther (whom see never met), her near-obsession with WWII, and her recurring nightmares (which began at age 4) which she spontaneously endorsed as a kind of memory of my father’s being shot down in Nazi Germany; I also mentioned that she has issues with anxiety and defiance.

You replied that I might want to consider the possibility that she might actually be my father reincarnated.

My initial reaction was disquietude, and it’s been hard to hold that possibility in mind with enough equanimity to reflect constructively. But I am now working with this idea in several ways: First, I simply call to mind the thought that she could be my re-incarnated father, holding it in mind when we are conversing, or even just hanging out. While I don’t yet have enough track record to discern a clear result, I sense a our tension has ratcheted down a notch, as she is at least willing to vent anger at me and listen to my response rather than just stomp out, or tune out. I have begun a second approach, writing a biography of my father. The only source of information she has about him is what I tell her and a few pictures, so I thought a written biography might interest her. I’ve spent a few hours on it so far, and this morning I mentioned I was writing it for her. She perked right up and said “Thank you”, something she rarely says unprompted or with sincerity, certainly to me. Finally, when I do broach the topic, I will do so only after she has read the biography, and I see her reaction to it; I wonder if it might ‘jog her memory’ as it were. Even then, I will mention reincarnation as one possibility among several, the other being the idea of inherited trauma; which has been on my mind, and there a book, “It Didn’t Start With You. How Inherited Family Trauma Shapes Who We are and How to End the Cycle”, by Mark Wolyn, that I have started reading.

So JMG, does this seem like a constructive approach? I am interested in anything you might have to suggest.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation continued
replies.content: That sounds very much worth trying. I suspect it will work well.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation continued
replies.content:
If you don't mind me asking: how typical do you consider yourself about being able to write a biography of your father?

- S. T. Silva.

replies.title: Re: Reincarnation continued
replies.content:
Hmm…, I’m not quite sure I understand what you mean by “typical”. I’ll try to give a better answer if you clarify. I do think I am a fairly capable writer, at least in the sense of putting together a coherent, even lucid narrative. In a medical context, when I’m writing notes I intend to be used by my patients’ attorneys, I get high marks for lucidity and persuasiveness. There are attorneys who send patients to me for this reason alone.

As for my dad’s bio, there is recollection, selection, reconstruction, filling in gaps, editing… It is a real chore actually. But I think I'm doing good job, and I am trying to help my daughter...

comments.title: Alternative paths
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

I tried to post this a couple weeks ago but I don't think it made it through your screening (or b/c of timezones, I may have posted it too late), so forgive me for asking again.

In one of your early posts before you started the Cosmic Doctrine study thread, you mentioned that becoming a mage was just one of the magical paths available. I'm wondering if you can elaborate on the alternatives as I'm trying to figure out what is the right course for me to chart.

I'm attracted to magic by Crowley's "change of consciousness in accordance with will" definition. There are a few ways I would like to change my consciousness in a personal growth sort of way. This is related to issues of love/self-love and acceptance/self-acceptance. To this end, I started a practice of daily LBRP followed by meditation or journaling. I've had some success and want to deepen my practice but am not sure where to go. Additionally, I have start on Cosmic Doctrine, but am struggling to fully grasp Chp 2. I definitely feel that it has something to teach me.

1) Should I continue daily LBRP or should I alternate with LIRP?
2) Is there a middle-pillar-like ritual I should start to include?
3) What are some alternatives to the mage path I should consider?

Lastly, I'm vegetarian (totally healthy and feel great) and I saw you mention "mental sensitivity" as a possible consequence. Does that affect any of the answers to 1)-3) or am I still at the level of dabbling that it isn't that important?

Thank you for all the wisdom you put out into the world!
Alex

replies.title: Re: Alternative paths
replies.content:
1) Depends on what you want to do and where you want to go.

2) Only if you want to take up the magical path. If you want to continue with your vegetarian diet, I don't recommend this.

3) The basic dichotomy in occultism is between the mage and the mystic. The mage works with subtle energy and the will; the mystic works with subtle perception and the heart. If the mystic way appeals to you, you might consider looking into the Theosophical society, on the one hand, or the Rosicrucian Fellowship on the other -- both of these offer extensive training in mysticism and both include a vegetarian diet as a basic element of practice, so you ought to find them congenial.

replies.title: Re: Alternative paths
replies.content: Not the original poster, but I'm curious here: are these really a dichotomy? It seems like there could be plenty of ways to mix the two, but maybe it's possible that they reliably crash and burn. Do you know sources that discuss the region between the two?

replies.title: Re: Alternative paths
replies.content: It's standard for occultists in the old-fashioned schools to do a little of both, and then specialize, but they require different modes of training and development, so it's very uncommon for people to get far with both. I don't know of any general sources on this, though some of Dion Fortune's essays talk about it.

comments.title: Working with Golden Dawn magic
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer. Thanks for hosting this space, very generous of you.

I have a little acquaintance with Golden Dawn magic having followed the course in your co-authored book 'Learning Ritual Magic' and I'm interested in going deeper. I've picked up a copy of Israel Regardie's 'The Golden Dawn' but I'm not sure exactly how to approach working with the material. Specifically, I'm wary of working through the programme in a linear fashion, approximating the path initiates of the original Golden Dawn order would have taken, because of something I remember reading you say on the comment thread of one of your blog posts, to the effect that the original Golden Dawn programme has certain imbalances which readily lead adherents' egos to balloon to monstrous sizes. My question, then, is what amendments do you recommend making to the material in Regardie's book in order to make it more balanced and safer? Or is it less a matter of making substantive changes and more about having reasonable expectations? For instance, I believe you pointed to the equation of the initiation rituals with the Sephiroth as one potential pitfall; is it enough just to know that performing, say, the Tiphereth ritual doesn't automatically mean you are going to acquire Tiphereth consciousness?

Relatedly, I'm wondering whether you think it's important, if one wishes to use Golden Dawn magic in a manner both effective and safe, to follow the original programme as closely as possible, making only the amendments that are strictly necessary (assuming there are at least some), or do you think one might equally take a freer, more syncretic approach, incorporating rituals and practices that have been developed as the Golden Dawn tradition has evolved through time but aren't part of the original programme while referring to the original programme itself as a resource rather than something to be followed to the letter? In other words, how anal do I have to be about it?!

Thanks again, Albert

replies.title: Re: Working with Golden Dawn magic
replies.content: What I recommend is that once you finish the course of training in Learning Ritual Magic, you proceed to my book Circles of Power as the basis for your further training. Meanwhile read Regardie's The Golden Dawn from cover to cover, and use the knowledge lectures, rituals, and other teaching as the raw material for your daily meditations. By the time you've finished working through that you'll be able to pick and choose intelligently from the material in the broader Golden Dawn tradition.

replies.title: Re: Working with Golden Dawn magic
replies.content: Lovely.  Just the question I was formulating for a future Magic Monday, neatly answered.  --Sidney

replies.title: Re: Working with Golden Dawn magic
replies.content: Thanks, much obliged! I'll pick up a copy of Circles of Power when I can. Albert.

comments.title: Intelligences in Agrippa
comments.content:
My little study group, which worked our way through the Picatrix a couple years ago, then the PGM, has just started on Agrippa's Three Books. I'm not very far in yet, maybe 100 pages or so.

I'm particularly working on understanding a couple of passages. The first passage is in Chapter VIII p. 27 in the hardcover Tyson edition, in which he says, "Elements therefore in the exemplary world are Ideas of things to be produced, in intelligences are distributed powers, in heavens are virtues, and in inferior bodies gross forms."

The other passage is in Chapter XIII (p. 39) in which he says that the virtue of things comes from Ideas, then the ruling and governing intelligences, then the heavens, then the elements.

One of our goals for this group is to let our studies direct our magical practices, and this sequence seems like an Agrippan formula for manifestation, or for stepping spiritual influences down into the physical level.

I could just say, well, that seems like the qabalistic four worlds and leave it at that, but I want to understand Agrippa and the uniqueness of his ideas.

My question is what are the intelligences? Somewhere he says that the reader should understand them through the study of theology, but I tried to search for the idea online, and maybe due to bad searching skills, only found references to the Book of Mormon, so I feel like that was off track.

In the paragraph previous to the one I cited in Ch. XIII, Agrippa mentions them as servants of God. The annotation Tyson gives to that paragraph references Plato, who calls the Intelligences the offspring of the Divine.

I also find the term "distributed powers" from p. 27 evocative. Working with that, it seems that Ideas would be abstract concepts, and with "distributed powers" I get the sense of movement. I want to associate the concept "verb" to the intelligences, that they are the verbs, the actions, maybe even a "primum mobile" type of concept. That they initiate the sequence of events that moves the abstraction of the Ideas down through the other levels.

Where I get confused is that there is an appendix with the planetary magic squares. I'm not sure if the appendices were in the original material or if the editor added those. Of course, I know about the plantary spirits and intelligences. But is this actually what Agrippa is referencing, or is he using the word in a different way?

Also, one of the first things that came to mind as I pondered this idea is the Intelligences that are part of the yetziratic texts for the paths. Again, I suspect that he's not referencing this, but I also don't want to overlook anything obvious.

So, can you briefly explain what his idea of the intelligences are? And/or point me towards a good starting place for further study and research? Also, I recognize I'm not very far into the book, so maybe I just need to keep reading and all will be revealed.

Thank you!

Joy Vernon

replies.title: Re: Intelligences in Agrippa
replies.content: In medieval and Renaissance Neoplatonism, the intelligences are a class of spiritual being exactly equivalent to the angels of Christian tradition. The Ideas are the gods; the Intelligences are their angels; the heavens are the containers by which their forces are distributed upon the earth; and the "inferior bodies" are material things that exist here on the plane of the four elements. The seven planetary intelligences are seven of these beings, and each one is served by a planetary spirit.

Another question JMG: I think I have a severe problem with regard to a healthy will. It manifests in several ways, one being procrastination, particularly for tasks that are high priority but low urgency (like jumping through all the arbitrary hoops you have to jump through over a period of years to maintain medical board certification). Other ways it shows up is difficulty staying on top of my work load, in keeping organized, such as with records, or keeping space at home and office tidy and functional, or organizing my time.

But there is another deep, existential way this deficiency shows up that I’ve only recently been able to describe, even to myself. I can best describe it by way of contrast with an extraordinary 4-year period of my life that happened 24 years ago: At that time, I felt, emotionally, that I had reached the end of the line with my engineering career (I was 36 at the time). I agonized over what else I might do, and finally, one Friday, settled on nursing. That evening, I visited my girlfriend, and as we sat at supper, I announced my decision to become a nurse. She was aghast; she put down her fork and knife, and said “What? You should be a doctor!”. That remark was an explosion that shattered a door from behind which a load of memories, inclinations and forgotten fantasies came tumbling out: I saw that I had long wanted to be a doctor, but had been afraid of the commitment and possibility of failure. Now it just so happened that my father had been deceased for 3 years, and one effect his passing had on me was to clear away the fears and objections I had had to medicine, but I did not realize this until my girlfriend raised the idea. From that moment, life was transformed: Within 72 hours, I had done my homework, and learned everything I had to do to become a doctor (and this was before the internet era). As I commuted home from work the following Monday, I heard a radio ad from the community college announcing there was still time to enroll for the fall term, so I drove straight to that college because I needed to earn credit for biology, enrolled on the spot, then walked to the classroom to make sure I could find it, and the instructor happened to be there. He and I had this great rapport, and he became my advisor for the process of getting into med school. And within several weeks, I also found a new engineering job in the biomedical industry, which was not only an improvement from my prior job, but would look relevant on a medical school application… and this amazing stuff went on and on and on…

The moment I realized I wanted to get into medicine, it was as if I had mounted a winning racehorse that then carried me at breakneck speed past all obstacles. It was almost effortless. Over 4 years, it carried me non-stop all the way to medical school, and I thought I would have my ‘racehorse’ for the rest of my life. But it vanished after depositing me at the medical school door (notwithstanding several brief returns when I discovered my clinical subspecialty, and again for my medical research which would win a major award, get published). Without my racehorse, I have been slogging through waist deep mud for most of these last 20 years. I have come perilously close to failure several times.

That ‘racehorse’ must have been my will. It was extraordinary, amazing beyond description; only Pegasus might compare. It came suddenly out of nowhere, propelled me to achievements that were not in the cards, then left me high and dry. I don’t know how or why this all happened. I suspect I accidentally did something right magically at the beginning; or perhaps a non-physical being intervened, then left. Who knows? I’m emotionally spent and struggling. (My condition can’t be helping with my daughter, as I’ve mentioned in another line of questioning.)

So JMG, first, does this make sense to you? I would sure like to get back in that saddle. What would you recommend? Where would you begin?

replies.title: Re: but Is there no course of training of the will?

comments.title: Hooodoo Bath / Evil Eye Ritual Parallels
comments.content:
Hi, JMG. Thanks for hosting these weekly Magic Mondays: you are rendering an invaluable service.

Quite a few times over the past several months there have been discussions about hoodoo baths, which was an education for me. Just recently I was reflecting on it and I remembered a ritual which is commonly used by South Asians to cure people of the “evil eye” (negative thoughts from others, often based on envy or jealousy). The version that I am familiar with involves the “victim” sitting under a doorframe, with a person holding a pot of water in the right hand and with salt and a whole dry chili pepper in the left hand placed over the water pot. The water/salt/chili combination is then rotated clockwise around the victim’s head and then in front, back and both sides of the person. Following this, the water, salt and chili are discarded outside the property in a way so that the water flows away from the property.

Obviously there are many similarities between the hoodoo bath and evil eye ritual. However, vinegar is not in the evil eye ritual, as it is virtually non-existent in traditional Indian villages.

I suspect that the evil eye ritual is not as powerful as the hoodoo bath due to the fact that in the former there is no physical contact between the “victim” and these substances. I do, however, attest to the efficacy of the evil eye ritual when properly conducted.

I would just like to know if you have heard of such rituals that resemble the hoodoo bath and the degree of similarities among them. It looks like several cultures have found a common form of natural magic to cure people of similar types of “contamination”. Any comments?

Ron M

replies.title: Re: Hooodoo Bath / Evil Eye Ritual Parallels
replies.content: Interesting. Yes, that would work; the water and salt would absorb etheric crud, and the pepper would tend to disrupt unwanted patterns. There are a lot of such rituals; my assumption is that they're so popular in so many cultures because they work.

comments.title: Some thoughts RE: yoga, caffeine and alcohol
comments.content:
Dear JMG and others,

I've practiced yoga regularly for about 20 years. While I am no master and practice primarily the hatha yoga (physical exercise), I have had many teachers from different schools (Iyengar, Ashtanga, Anusara, Vinyasa) and I've learned a few things about prana along the way.

I decided to pay closer attention to what was really happening in my yoga classes, and then experiment on myself with coffee afterwards. Observations:

1. The yoga classes I have taken in the US have been diverse in physical style, but uniformly emphasize compassion and its physical equivalent, opening the heart center of the body. Most of the time compassion is taught to start with the self; everyone needs to be compassionate to themselves, and then compassion will flow to other creatures as well. In general (although I've met the unpleasant exception on occasion) most people coming out of yoga classes do have true compassion flowing from them.

2. If I drink coffee after yoga class, I find that my perception of physical pain ratchets up and the flow of compassion clamps down. It takes about an hour to kick in after ingesting the coffee, and lasts for 6 to 8 hours afterwards.

So it's possible that people doing yoga and having this flow of compassion can clamp it down in an unpleasant way that emphasizes pain and irritation by drinking coffee. I don't think this accounts for the insanity gripping much of the US, though. That seems to be in the astral level (I've been noticing it in my dreams a lot. Anger. Lots and lots of anger.)

Now for alcohol: I never drink alcohol close enough to yoga to do a similar observational experiment, but in thinking about it, something related came to mind: I used to row competitively. For those of you unfamiliar with the sport, it usually involves 4 to 8 very large, powerful people (the crew) rowing in sync, obeying the commands of a tiny, strong willed person who steers (the coxswain). When a crew team is working together at its best, they move as a single being, rowing and even breathing together and subsuming their individual wills to that of the boat as a whole, channeled by the coxwain. I never thought about the simultaneous breathing exercises we did then as anything more than timing exercises, but it occurs to me now that we were channeling prana (life energy) in a yoga kinda way...

Anyway, point being that after good rows, we'd all get drunk really easily and often spectacularly. And I'm wondering if the joint prana flow had anything to do with that, and maybe these are the "colorful mental states" mentioned by someone a few weeks ago.

-Ms. Krieger

replies.title: Re: Some thoughts RE: yoga, caffeine and alcohol
replies.content: Interesting! Thank you for this; it makes a lot of sense.

comments.title: Occult Dream?
comments.content:
Hello John Michael,

Thank you for providing this forum for discussion. I have a question about dream interpretation. Before I get to the dream, let me say that I have read Man and His Symbols (Jung, et. al.), and found it quite illuminating. I also realize that most dreams are sort of cleaning out your mental lint-trap, a few may have personal symbolic meaning, and a very few may have deeper occult meaning. I believe that I had one of the latter sort about 18-20 years ago, and have not been able to understand it. Perhaps you or someone else here might have some insight. Note that I was 30-ish when I had this dream, and had been out of school for some time.

The dream:
I am walking on a concrete walkway high up on the side of a building. The building is to my left, and there is a roughly 4-foot wall at the edge of the walkway to my right, so no-one falls off. The building is a dormitory at the university I attended, and the real one does have walkways somewhat like the one I am on. It is early morning, just before dawn. I am up high enough that I mostly see endless treetops stretching out in all directions. The air is slighly misty and suffused with an orange glow from the coming dawn. I know that the sunrise will be in front of me, so I am walking east. I reach the corner of the building and turn to my left, now going south. There are dorm room doors and windows that I pass, but all the lights are off, and I do not try to enter any of them. The wall at the edge of the path slopes down and then goes away so that the part of the walkway I am on now has no barrier at the edge. It is silent, but I notice something, a vibration or form of sound different than ordinary sound, as a low rumble. (Yes I know silence and noise are mutually exclusive, but that's how the dream went. I wonder if that is what synethesia feels like - hearing some other sense-impression?) The sun breaks above the horizon. The "silent noise" gets more pronounced. A moment later, I see a second sun rising just after the first. I begin to feel a gentle tug towards the suns (and off the unprotected edge of the path). The dream speeds up at this point. More suns rise, they just keep coming. The "noise" gets louder. The pull gets stronger. I have to walk at an angle to counteract the pull and stay on the path. The final scene of the dream, when all the suns have come up (I know this in the dream), has me pulled horizontal by the force of the suns, holding on by my fingertips to a gap between the concrete path and the building wall, the the strange noise, a deafening, but still silent, roar, feeling like it's shaking the entire planet, and the suns blazing in the pattern that they have risen in. I don't remember the exact number of suns, but the pattern looked like this:

.....................................*
.................................*.......*
................................*.........*
.................................*.......*
.....................................*

...............................*

..........................*
......................*.......*
.....................*.........*
......................*.......*
..........................*

(it is a pattern of two circles of suns on a diagonal, with one sun between them; ignore the periods, they were necessary to make it come out properly in the comment)

At that point, I woke up, shaking, and believing that something, my subconscious or something else, was trying to send a message. It was a powerful enough feeling that I still get some of it when I remember the dream even now. Unfortunately, I did not know what this sign (and probably also symbol) meant when I dreamed it. I have recently discovered that it looks a lot like the astrological/astronomical symbol for conjunction, but I still don't know what the dream means. Any insight would be most appreciated. Thanks!
-Steve

replies.title: Re: Occult Dream?
replies.content: I have no talent for dream interpretation. It sounds like an interesting dream, though, and I'd recommend meditating on it one image or detail at a time to see what you can get from it.

replies.title: Re: Occult Dream?
replies.content:
OK. I'll give that a try. Maybe divination would help too.

Thanks.

-Steve

replies.title: Re: Occult Dream?
replies.content:
Uh. Hmm.

That just might be a pantheon's symbol. You mentioned that you don't remember the number of suns, but that would go double if there were twelve in each ring. Somebody on JMG's other blog (Robert Mathiesen?) mentioned a while back that the classic Spiritualist circle was twelve man/woman pairs surrounding a central medium; one of my own core pieces of symbolism is similar, with a dead-living god pair mediating between two sets of twelve male-female deity pairs arranged in parallel rings. I've usually divided the outer rings into nine outer pairs and three inner pairs making an inscribed triangle, but I might be wrong about that (would be consistent with a couple of pieces of mental imagery) - and what you're describing sounds a lot like "two parallel rings with a central point linking them" projected into 2D.

(The goddess I associate with the middle point is the one who hijacked one of my meditation sessions for a sit-me-down once, and I also sat up and took notice when Irma's name on the hurricane name list got replaced with a rather odd choice [Idalia] that's really similar to my usual name for one the goddesses I'd place in the upper inscribed triangle [Idalye]...)

My impression is that the pantheon in question is either new or possibly a resurrected old pantheon. Either way, that offers a blatantly obvious possible partial interpretation that may or may not be correct: "there's a new pantheon rising, and the rising is shaking the world".

Or it could be something else entirely. YMMV.

(Also FWIW, the imagery of a mostly-uninterrupted canopy of trees stretching from the Appalachians to the Mississippi and south to the Ozark/Ouachita line is one that's come to my mind before, and my gut reaction to the final scene is "sounds like the sort of thing one has to interpret oneself".)

replies.title: Re: Occult Dream?
replies.content:
If you fix a camera in place, and take a picture of the sky every day at the same time for a whole year, and then print all those multiple images together, you'll get a figure that looks very much like what you've pictured here. Don't know how that relates to your dream, but you can't have too many clues, right?

There is also Jung's Green Christ vision, which is not related, I think, but that you might want to check.

replies.title: Re: Green Christ?
replies.content:
Most of what I've encountered with regard to the Green Christ has been by way of quiet discussions among Druids, usually prefaced by carefully evasive questions to make sure nobody's going to freak out about the use of Christian symbolism. I don't know of much of anything in print.

The basic idea is that the first chapter of the Gospel of John sets out a way of understanding Christ that Christian churches basically haven't followed up on. According to that chapter, Christ, the Word or Logos, is the person of the Trinity that brought all of nature into being: "All things were made by him, and without him was not any thing made that was made. In him was life, and the life was the light of men." In effect, the creation of the world was the First Coming of Christ, and everything in nature is his direct creation. (The incarnation was the Second Coming, and Christians wait now for the Third Coming.) So every natural thing, every tree and blade of grass and animal and fish and wind and sunset and everything else in nature, is a revelation of the Christ; all life is in him, and that life provides the light by which human beings can find their way.

replies.title: Re: Green Christ?
replies.content: Fascinating. I think I've had experiences of the Green Christ, or something like him, by way of Anglican practice (in Canada).

replies.title: Re: Green Christ?
replies.content:
This was the standard Medieval Christian interpretation on the fist chapters of Genesis: the God whose creating activity is described there is the second Hypostasis [Person] in the Trinity (that is, God the Son), not the first Hypostasis (God the Father). Pretty much every Medieval illustration we have of the seven days of Creation depicts Christ as the Creator. See, for example, this magnificent example from the "Bible Moralisée":

en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:God_the_Geometer.jpg

(The form of the halo, and also the garments, codes the Creator as God the Son.)

comments.content:
Hello and thank you for all you do.

I'm very seriously considering diving into the DMH. I like the system overall and it seems a very good fit for my purposes, except for one crucial component: I don't resonate with the Ogham.

I remember you said at least once that people seem to resonate with either the runes or the ogham but not both. I definitely feel a potent connection to the runes, but haven't found a complete magical system incorporating them that I really like.

My main concern is putting a bunch of work into DMH (I have rather limited time and energy) and it leading nowhere, because it's the wrong magical alphabet.

Is this something that can be overcome with enough work, study, and perseverance? Or will my divinations come out flat and my meditations lead in circles?

~Grey

replies.title: Re: Ogham and DMH
replies.content: If you're going to do a system of magical training, do it exactly as given, even if you don't think some part of it is a good fit. In The Druid Magic Handbook, Ogham plays a relatively modest role; what's more, whether you resonate immediately with a symbolic system doesn't necessarily predict whether you will get any benefit from it. If you want to do the DMH system, do it, and then after you've completed the training, return to the runes and figure out, on the basis of your experience, how to adapt the system to include them.

comments.content:
I know someone who had a near death experience and has seemed "off" since. It's like he's a different person. People keep saying it's a result of the brain damage, but I'm slightly psychic and feel a difference beyond that.

I've come across this idea of "walk ins" where a soul leaves the body but another one attaches itself to the body. How common is it, and how can I tell if that's what's going on?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: I've never been sure how seriously to take the notion of "walk-ins". Everyone I've met who claimed to be one turned out to be a flake. That said, it's not impossible that that's what's going on. How to tell? Barring a very well-developed clairvoyant sense, there isn't much that will do so.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Thank you for taking the time to answer. Since I don't have a well developed clairvoyant sense, I guess I just have to do my best to work with things as they are.

replies.title: Walkins
replies.content:
One way to tell is to look at the astrology of the walk-in incident. If it's valid, it ought to be a valid birth chart that will react to transits and progressions just like the original birth chart.

John Roth

replies.title: Re: Walkins
replies.content: That's a very good point.
